data.title: Buy Certara After The FDA Announcement
data.description:
On Thursday, April 10, 2025, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration announced that it would phase out animal testing requirements for monoclonal antibody therapies and other drugs. Known as mAB for short, this type of therapy gradually replaced steroid-based treatments. In addition, mAB-based drugs have relatively fewer side effects. As a result, the FDA is advancing drug testing methods to embrace the latest technologies. This includes the use of artificial intelligence, data-driven modeling, and predictive simulation.

In reaction to the news, Certara (CERT) jumped by 15%. Will the new regulatory environment help the company increase its growth rate? Investors should look first at Certara’s fourth-quarter results.

Growth in the Fourth Quarter

In the last quarter, Certara reported non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.15. Revenue increased by 14.1% Y/Y to $100.4 million. For the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, however, it reported a loss of $12.05 million. This is down from a $55.36 million loss in FY 2023.

In Q4, the EBITDA adjustments included intangible asset amortization of $17.5 million and equity-based compensation of $7.7 million. The adjusted net income of $24.7 million deducted amortization of adjusted-related intangible assets at $14.4 million and equity-based compensation (also $7.73 million).

Chief Executive Officer William F. Feehery commented that the core biosimulation and Chemaxon businesses contributed to the company’s results. The CEO said that Certara would continue its investments in software in AI, which would strengthen its “end-to-end biosimulation offering that spans from drug discovery through the clinic.”

Chemaxon is a software solution that Certara acquired last year. The cheminformatics company provides “software solutions for property calculation and molecule design, chemical drawing, chemical search, and compound data management.”

AI As an Assistant

Certara did not hype AI by too much. It mentioned the term “AI” eight times on the conference call and (generative) “artificial intelligence” twice. Fortunately, the FDA announcement is a tailwind to Certara’s business strategy. The firm positioned the growth around capitalizing on the growing interest in biosimulation technology.

Investors may infer that the investments, which weighed on operating earnings, will pay off. The FDA news increases the attractiveness of holding CERT stock. In addition, readers may renew their interest in Schrödinger (SDGR). This firm sells scientific software to customers who develop computational tools in drug discovery and material science (according to the company’s ‘About us’ page).

Readers may also consider Ginkgo Bioworks (DNA) since they have an AI focus. However, Certara has a practical and cautious approach to how it spends on AI to enhance its product.

2025 Outlook

Before the FDA announcement, Certara issued a 2025 revenue forecast of between $415 million to $425 million. EBITDA margin will be in the range of 30-32%. At an adjusted EPS of $0.42 - $0.46, CERT stock trades at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of as low as 26.5 times.

Readers may compare Certara’s returns, Seeking Alpha Quant rating, and factor grades on this page. The default tickers include Schrödinger, Teladoc (TDOC), and Phreesia (PHR). In the table below, CERT stock ranks below that of its peers except for Evolent Health (EVH).

Investors should consider CERT stock more attractive than Teladoc. Shorts are betting heavily against the firm with a 15.3% short interest. The share price is “fading” to the downside because of the expensive acquisition of Livongo for $37 billion in 2020. Ever since the COVID lockdown ended and people chose to visit their doctor in person, Livongo’s costs weighed on its balance sheet.

Your Takeaway

The FDA is a tailwind for Certara. Previously reported GAAP losses should turn to break-even earnings as biotechnology firms adapt computational models. Compared to using animal models, this should give them faster results related to drug interactions and safety.

CERT stock is an attractive, speculative buy. However, wait for speculators to take profits in their position in the coming days. That would lower the share price and give investors a better entry price.

data.title: Built For The Tough Times: QHY's Edge In Today's High-Yield Market
data.description:
By Behnood Noei, CFA

Following last week's sharp sell-off in risk assets, investors have sought refuge in safer assets such as U.S. Treasury bonds. As a naturally more volatile segment of the fixed income market, high-yield (HY) bond spreads have widened by 92 basis points relative to Treasuries since April 2, 2025, "Liberation" Day." However, the sell-off hasn't been uniform across the HY spectrum. BB-rated bonds - considered the higher-quality tier within the HY space - saw spreads widen by 73 basis points, while the more speculative CC-rated bonds experienced a significantly steeper widening of 153 basis points.

Figure 1: Option-Adjusted Spread (OAS)

For many investors, fixed income plays a supporting role in their portfolios—not as a primary driver of returns, but as a source of diversification and stability during periods of market stress. That's why maintaining a quality bias within a fixed income allocation is so important. In this piece, we'll take a closer look at the WisdomTree U.S. High Yield Corporate Bond Fund (QHY) and explore how its focus on higher-quality bonds, relative to both its peers and the broader Index, has contributed to its performance.

Higher Quality When It Matters

QHY has historically maintained a higher allocation to higher-quality bonds - and that's by design. Our investment approach, grounded in both fundamental and quantitative analysis, is intentionally structured to avoid lower-quality, potentially riskier issuers. As of the end of Q1, QHY held an over-weight position of approximately 9% in BB-rated bonds, while remaining under-weight in lower-rated segments such as B and below.

Figure 2: Ratings

This emphasis on higher-quality bonds has been reflected in QHY's performance. Year-to-date, and since April 2, 2025, the Fund has outperformed the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index by 62 and 10 basis points, respectively.

Figure 3: Relative Performance

A Timely Change

In our most recent rebalance (November 2024), we introduced equity return momentum as an additional factor to complement and enhance the long-term free cash flow signals in our model. This momentum metric evaluates a bond issuer's equity performance against other public issuers in the U.S. high-yield corporate debt universe. These momentum scores are then combined with the issuer's fundamental metrics to refine the bond selection process. The primary goal of this enhancement was to gain a timelier view of an issuer's financial health—and to further elevate the Fund's overall quality by identifying and excluding potential problem issuers before their fundamentals begin to weaken. A rough estimate suggests this change has contributed more than nine basis points in additional performance versus the previous methodology, underscoring the quality boost provided by incorporating momentum.

Figure 4: Momentum

Conclusion

In times of market stress, weaker issuers tend to get hit the hardest. That's where a steady focus on quality can make a real difference. QHY has shown how a thoughtful mix of fundamental research and timely quantitative insights—like the recent addition of equity momentum—can help it steer clear of the riskiest issuers. By tilting toward higher-quality bonds, QHY has delivered a better performance through recent volatility. For investors looking to stay invested in high yield without taking on unnecessary risk, a quality-focused approach like QHY may offer a more resilient way forward.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Fixed income investments are subject to interest rate risk; their value will normally decline as interest rates rise. High-yield or “junk” bonds have lower credit ratings and involve a greater risk to principal. Fixed income investments are also subject to credit risk, the risk that the issuer of a bond will fail to pay interest and principal in a timely manner or that negative perceptions of the issuer’s ability to make such payments will cause the price of that bond to decline. While the Fund attempts to limit credit and counterparty exposure, the value of an investment in the Fund may change quickly and without warning in response to issuer or counterparty defaults and changes in the credit ratings of the Fund’s portfolio investments. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

You cannot invest directly in an index.

Behnood Noei, CFA, Director, Fixed Income

Behnood Noei serves as Director of Fixed Income at WisdomTree Asset Management, where he develops the firm’s suite of fixed income and currency exchange-traded funds and enhances existing investment processes. Behnood has 11 years investment experience in portfolio management and quantitative research. Prior to joining WisdomTree in 2022, Behnood was a portfolio manager and developer of some of the fixed income ETFs at J.P.Morgan Asset Management, where he was directly responsible for managing more than 7 Fixed Income ETFs and multiple SMAs with more than $13Billion in assets. He graduated from The Ohio State University with Master of Science degree in Finance and is a CFA charter holder.

data.title: The Gold Standard: 5 Gold Mining Stocks To Dig Up Big Returns
data.description:
Trump’s Trade War & Falling US Dollar Unlocks a New Gold Rush

Grab your shovel. There’s a new kind of gold rush happening on Wall Street.

Maintaining a well-balanced commodities portfolio can be worth its weight in gold in terms of mitigating risk in volatile markets. Gold prices rose more than 1% on Friday, extending a multi-day rally as an escalating trade war between the U.S. and China, tariff fears, and a weakening U.S. dollar drove investors towards the safe-haven metal. ING Bank NV strategist Francesco Pesole wrote:

The question of a potential dollar confidence crisis has now been definitively answered – we are experiencing one in full force…The dollar collapse is working as a barometer of ‘sell America’ at the moment

Over the past year, gold has emerged as a favored safe-haven asset as investors seek to safeguard their portfolios from heightened volatility and rising trade tensions. In March, it exceeded $3,000 per troy ounce for the first time—its biggest price milestone since the COVID-19 pandemic.

Gold Prices Vault Past $3,000 (as of 4/11/25)

According to an analysis by the World Gold Council, a strengthening euro, economic uncertainty triggered by President Donald Trump’s back-and-forth tariff policies, and rising demand for Gold ETFs all propelled gold prices to record highs in March.

Tariff Fears and a Weakening Dollar Push Gold Price to New Highs

U.S. ETFs came in gold with $6B of net inflows, followed by Europe and Asia with 1B apiece. Meanwhile, a sweeping sell-off in equities fueled by tariff uncertainty further drove demand for gold in March.

Gold ETF Flows Surge in 2025

Last month, I recommended readers consider one of two Strong Buy Gold ETFs: the iShares® Gold Trust Micro ETF (IAUM) or SPDR® Gold MiniShares ETF (GLDM), depending on their preferred investment size. Both continue to eclipse the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY), which closely mirrors the S&P 500. Since recommending these funds on March 19, both have gained around 3.8% compared to SPY’s near 8.5% loss as of April 10.

SA Quant’s ETF Picks Crush the Broader Market

Looking ahead, softer-than-expected inflation data released on Thursday has prompted Fed futures traders to bet that the Federal Reserve will resume slashing rates in June, according to the CME FedWatch tool. Traders have also upped their expectations that the Fed would cut interest rates by 75 basis points in 2025 to a full percentage point by the end of the year. Slashed interest rates typically result in lower borrowing costs, which tend to benefit non-yielding assets such as gold.

82% of Fed Futures Traders Predict a June Rate Cut (as of 4/11/25)

Equities valuations remain high, forecasting further downside to equities should the economic slowdown continue. After the Trump administration’s sudden 90-day pause on most tariffs this week, the market exploded, but the euphoria quickly reversed again. While the tariff freeze may have prevented severe damage to the economy, concerns linger over the levies’ long-term effects. “We believe even these reduced tariffs will imply a serious hit to growth,” the UBS global research team warned on Thursday morning as the U.S. stock market retreated once again following Wednesday’s epic rally.

Amid this gloomy outlook for U.S. stocks, gold shines even brighter. “I think we’re very close, if not in, a recession now,” BlackRock (BLK) CEO Larry Fink told CNBC on Friday, adding that he did not think the U.S. was in a financial crisis and that “megatrends” in the economy like artificial intelligence would persist. J.P. Morgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon said Friday that he expects “estimates for corporate earnings to fall amid the uncertainty created by President Donald Trump’s trade negotiations.”

The comments from the leaders of the world’s largest financial institutions paint a fairly bleak picture for the economy in the near-term. Likewise, these comments can be viewed as positive for Gold’s continued rise. “We remain quite positive for gold,” Dominic Schnider, head of commodities and Asia Pacific currencies at UBS Global Wealth Management, told Bloomberg TV on Thursday. “The next step is going to be, at some point, the Fed coming in — and that gives the next leg up for gold.”

Major banks remain bullish that the price of gold will continue to rise: BofA’s head of metals research, Michael Widmer, predicts that the precious metal will rise to $3,500 in the long term, while Macquarie anticipates it will reach $3,500 by next quarter.

Investing in Gold Producers

Typically less correlated to traditional assets such as stocks and bonds, exposure to physical assets offers a myriad of benefits, from inflation protection to diversification to the potential for high returns.

As I've written before, gold ETFs can be a simple and effective way to gain broad exposure to the asset class. Alternatively, gold mining stocks can provide investors with exposure to the asset class, solid dividend income, and a layer of protection against market volatility. There are plenty of advantages to investing in gold producers. Unlike investing in the physical commodity, gold mining stocks often pay dividends, making them a compelling option for income-oriented investors seeking steady returns in volatile markets.

According to an analysis by DWS, gold mining stocks have grown more profitable in recent years after reducing their debt levels, increasing M&A, and placing a higher emphasis on cash generation. They even outperformed the price of gold for significant periods in 2024, a stark reversal of historical trends that shocked many industry insiders, as Reuters Breakingviews’ Edward Chancellor wrote.

Gold Mining Stocks Outperformed Gold on Several Occasions in 2024, Surprising Industry Insiders

Looking forward, Washington’s escalating trade war with China, lingering concerns over economic growth, and future interest rate cuts could potentially boost the performance of gold mining stocks even further. The SA Quant Team has five Strong Buy gold production stocks that have benefitted from gold’s extended rally, all of which possess strong balance sheets and excellent fundamentals.

We selected these stocks by reviewing the Top Gold Stocks quant screen. Our preferences were determined by focusing on Gold stocks that possess both solid growth metrics and an attractive valuation frameworks.

1. DRDGOLD Limited (DRD)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.3B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 1 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 1 out of 43

DRDGOLD is a South African mining group ranked first among all materials stocks and all gold stocks, producing gold from the retreatment of surface mine tailings. Headquartered in Johannesburg, the company commissioned one of the largest renewable energy initiatives in the industry by integrating solar photovoltaic (PV) technology and a renewable power grid into its operations to halve its usage of traditional energy sources.

DRD’s HY2025 results favorably reflected the rising gold price of gold, reporting a 28% increase in revenue and a 74% increase in operational profits to R1 578.7 million. Cash operating costs remained well-contained at R2 215.1 million. As SA analyst Alberto Abaterusso noted following its latest earnings results:

The outlook for DRDGOLD's profitability, a key driver of shares in the US stock market, is very promising as analysts continue to expect gold to be driven higher by strong demand for safe assets amid geopolitical risks and the Federal Reserve's rate cut. While gold is trading higher, the company is on track with higher throughput and lower costs.

DRD Stock Quant Breakdown

Surging gold prices are reflected by DRD’s ‘A+ momentum grade, with a three-month price performance of 56.2% compared to the materials sector median of -7.98%.

Its forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 13.2 is slightly cheaper than the sector at a -1.7% discount from the median, while its trailing price-to-earnings-growth (PEG) ratio of 0.48 similarly connotes a fair price tag. Profitability has improved from a ‘D’ to a ‘C’ over the past three months, boosted higher by return on total assets (TTM) of 16.6%, among other positive metrics.

In staying true to gold’s volatility-hedging qualities, DRD’s 1.4% dividend yield (TTM) offers steady, if modest, income with four years of consecutive payments. My next selection offers an even more attractive dividend yield.

2. AngloGold Ashanti plc (AU)

  • Market Capitalization: $19.6B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 15 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 7 out of 43

Formed in June 1998, AngloGold Ashanti (AU) is now one of the largest gold mining companies in the world, with operations in Africa, Australia, and the Americas. Last year, it completed its acquisition of Centamin, a gold mining and exploration company with mines in Egypt. This deal increased its annual gold production from 450,000 ounces to over 3M ounces, helping it surge more than 82% YTD. Its increased global footprint and the soaring price of gold helped it achieve record free cash flow last year.

Thanks to the Centamin transaction and strong tailwinds in the mining sector, AU has upped its production target this year to 2.90-3.23Moz, a 15% increase from 2024. As SA analyst Ivan Leyba recently wrote:

For this year, the company aims to surpass this milestone, which is why it has significantly increased its production target. This, coupled with the continued favorable macroeconomic environment for gold prices, could boost Anglo's operating and financial results to another level, helping to maintain the strong upward trend in its shares since the beginning of this year and putting it back on track to reach its highest levels of the last decade and a half.

AU Stock Quant Breakdown

AU’s biggest selling point is its 3.8% dividend yield, backed by an impressive five-year growth rate of 69%. It also boasts a strong dividend safety score, with a one-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 328%, an impressive 42,123% difference from the sector.

AU Dividend Safety Grade: ‘A-’

AU’s valuation has increased from a ‘D’ to a ‘C+’ over the past six months, with a forward P/E of 9.08, a 38% discount from the sector median. Growth is also strong, with forward revenue growth of 21.8%, a 665.5% difference from the 2.85% sector median.

Like AU, my next pick has drastically improved its production outlook, but its valuation remains cheap despite growing momentum.

3. SSR Mining Inc. (SSRM)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.06B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 4 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 2 out of 43

Headquartered in Denver, Colorado, SSR Mining (SSRM) produces gold, copper, silver, lead, and zinc across the United States, Canada, Turkey, and Argentina. Though its company's production guidance fell short of Wall Street expectations in Q4, shares are still up over 53% year-to-date as a result of the strong uptick in gold prices. It was recently upgraded by UBS after acquiring new Cripple Creek and Victor assets in Colorado, mitigating further downside from a heap leach failure at its Çöpler gold mine in Turkey.

A lapse in Turkish operations as a result of the Çöpler incident resulted in lower production in 2024. However, its powerful growth outlook and cheap valuation make it a compelling long-term investment. As SA analyst Taylor Dart observed:

SSR Mining is one of the better-run producers in the sector with disciplined capital allocation, a track record of per share growth, and several solid assets with diversification…However, the stock has been punished for being in an investment cycle with limited free cash flow generation this year and an outlook of lower production at higher costs. While this is certainly not the ideal setup…I think the negatives here are more than priced in and see this as a very attractive entry point for SSR Mining Inc. shares from a long-term investment standpoint while being paid to wait.

SSR Stock Quant Breakdown

Ranked second among all gold stocks, SSR’s Strong Buy status is upheld by excellent growth, strong momentum, and improving profitability at an attractive price. ‘A+’ momentum is reflected by three-month price performance of 34%, crushing the sector median by 9,984%.

Long-term EPS growth of 82.7%, a 680% difference from the sector median, underpins SSR’s recent acquisitions, increased production outlook, and the soaring price of gold amid intensifying demand. Profitability, which has improved from a ‘C+’ to a ‘B-’ over the past six months, is boosted by a levered free cash flow margin of 15%, an 189% difference from the sector. Finally, its forward PEG of 0.12, an 89% discount from the sector, reflects its strong growth outlook at an excellent price.

Similar to SSR, my next selection is a great choice for long-term, value-oriented investors looking for steady growth on the cheap.

4. IAMGOLD Corporation (IAG)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.9B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/10/25): 8 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/10/25): 4 out of 43

IAMGOLD Corporation (IAG), a Canadian miner headquartered in Toronto, produces gold across its home country and in Burkina Faso. The company has been leveling up production while bolstering its revenue growth since the opening of the Côté Gold mine, based in Ontario, last year.

After beginning commercial production in August 2024, the mine is expected to produce 250,000 to 280,000 ounces of gold in 2025, which IAG forecasts will result in a 33%-44% rise in production. That momentum has helped it surge more than 43% YTD as the rest of the market has struggled to stay in the green.

IAG February 2025 Presentation

IAG’s bullish growth outlook looks more attractive considering its low valuation, currently priced at just $6.88 per share. As SA analyst David Zanoni noted:

The stock's low valuation level leaves plenty of room to the upside for the stock. The combination of above-average revenue/earnings growth from a low valuation is a perfect recipe for the stock to outperform the broader market in 2025.

IAG Stock Quant Breakdown

IAG’s ‘A’ valuation grade is upheld by a top-tier forward P/E of 4.9, a 67% discount from the sector median. IAG’s low price tag comes with fantastic growth potential, with forward revenue growth of 38.2%, a 1,240% difference from the sector

Momentum is equally as powerful, with a six-month price performance of 43%, a 194% difference from the sector. With a net income margin (TTM) of 50% (a 1,003% difference from the sector) and return on total assets (TTM) of 15.25% (a 558% difference), IAG’s improving profitability is already shining through in the Quant metrics.

5. Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC)

  • Market Capitalization: $16.8B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials Stocks

  • Industry: Gold Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/10/25): 14 out of 276

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/10/25): 6 out of 43

Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) is a Canadian-based gold and silver miner operating six active gold mines across the U.S. (Nevada), Canada, Brazil, Chile, and Mauritania. Founded in 1993 and headquartered in Toronto, KGC’s Strong Buy rating is supported by its strong production, robust balance sheet, and high-quality assets in stable regions. The group reported strong 2024 results, achieving a record free cash flow of $1.34B, more than double the previous year.

The company reported an 8% quarterly decline in quarterly production, a drop-off stemming from dwindling supply at its Tasiast and Paracatu mines in Mauritania and Brazil. However, as KGC softens its production outlook for 2025, powerful industrial tailwinds more than make up for the difference. The Operating margins grew by 37% in Q4 compared to the 23% increase in the price of gold over the same period, taking full advantage of gold’s rapid price escalation.

KGC Stock Quant Breakdown

Despite its overall ‘D+’ valuation grade, KGC’s forward PEG of 14.2 comes at a near 5% discount to the sector. That could suggest that the miner’s shares are fairly priced when considering its long-term growth potential. Its forward P/E of 13.7 comes at a 1% premium to the sector. Growth appears strong across the board, with forward revenue growth of 10.5%, a 268.6% difference to the 7% sector median.

Momentum and profitability outshine the wider sector, with an ‘A+’ in both categories. KGC’s modest 0.92% dividend yield, while by no means the best in the industry, serves as an added bonus to its strong growth potential.

KGC, like the other four gold mining stocks discussed in this piece, make attractive options for investors looking to hedge their equity against volatility while gaining a layer of exposure to gold’s glistening rise.

Concluding Summary: A Golden Opportunity in Turbulent Markets

Gold has become a hot commodity for its inflation and volatility-hedging powers, and producers of the metals have profited immensely from its fantastic rise over the past year. The price of gold is likely to keep growing as the Fed looks to cut rates later this year and Trump’s 90-day pause on tariffs casts more uncertainty over the market.

The five Strong Buy mining stocks discussed in this piece can be a great way to diversify and mitigate downside risk as the broader market suffers in the face of economic uncertainty. Alternatively, if you’d like to gain broader exposure to the gold sector, consider using Seeking Alpha's ETF Ratings Screener tool to help find Strong Buy-rated gold ETFs.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: Developing Continuously, But Still Not Enough
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) has not performed well year over year, falling from $200 in mid-2024 to our current $88. In this article, I will explain in detail the reasons for this decline, what AMD needs to do to reverse its decline, and why I am currently a "Hold" for AMD stock.

Why Did Stock Fell?

The primary catalyst for AMD's decline was its lackluster performance in the AI ​​chip market, which Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) continues to dominate. But AMD's failure to compete even in the gaming market, a market NVDA no longer cares about, has also compounded this effect. For example, despite AMD's efforts to gain momentum with its MI300-series accelerators in recent months, the company has struggled to present a compelling value proposition against NVDA's established ecosystem.

Performance in the data center sector is also below expectations. AMD's data center segment revenue, which serves as a proxy for AI chip revenue, increased by 69% YoY in Q4 2024, reaching $3.86 billion. Even though this is a very good development, the company fell short of expectations, as the market expectation was $4.14 billion. While its closest competitor NVDA is constantly breaking expectations, AMD's failure to meet expectations in the same sector is unfortunately seen negatively by investors and constantly exposes the stock to selling pressure.

CEO Lisa Su then said data center sales will shrink by about 7% in the first quarter of 2025, which adds doubts about AMD's near-term AI growth trajectory. Su is optimistic about tens of billions in AI chip sales over the next few years, but the market wants immediate proof of success from the company. Unfortunately, AMD has not yet been successful in providing that.

Late Entry to AI

AMD's relatively late entry into the AI ​​acceleration market has been costly for the company. AMD has entered the market with both technological leadership and ecosystem advantages that are difficult to overcome. AMD's $5 billion in AI-related revenue in 2024 pales in comparison to NVDA's $100 billion in revenue in the same period.

Beyond its long-standing rivalry with NVDA, AMD also faces new competition from companies such as Arm Holdings, which is expanding its influence from smartphones to cloud computing and networking equipment.

Ecosystem Dominance Race

AMD can't produce cards as powerful as NVDA. But even if it does, the ecosystem is in NVDA's hands. The biggest reason for this is CUDA software. CUDA software continues to be a critical barrier for AMD in the AI ​​market. To compete against this, AMD has invested in ROCm software and partnered with AI infrastructure providers like TensorWave, which only uses MI300X accelerators. While I really see this move to create an alternative as a positive, its adoption is just beginning. So it's not possible to see the results in the short term.

In the desktop graphics card segment produced for end users and gamers, I see AMD slowly catching up with NVDA. Especially since NVDA has almost no competitors from GTX 1000 to RTX 2000, AMD's attempts to take over the budget graphics card market were somewhat successful. The fact that NVDA's performance, especially in the RTX 5000 series, was almost no different from the 4000 series and that there were stock shortages in the global market were a fiasco. This situation directed users looking for budget graphics cards to more affordable AMD models. Currently, the gaming market is in favor of AMD, but since AMD still does not have software as good as NVDA, the potential of graphics cards is not being used sufficiently.

There is a really big opportunity for AMD to make a breakthrough in this period where NVDA is completely focused on AI and not giving as much importance to the gaming side (especially the low-budget card segment). I hope they see this as soon as possible and take a bit more aggressive stance.

There is one technology market that NVDA has not entered and that is desktop CPUs. I see AMD very well in desktop CPUs and I congratulate them. Especially before AMD released the Ryzen series before 2017, INTC was the leader in desktop CPUs and the biggest criticisms AMD received were about thermal issues. After AMD listened to this problem and solved it, they made better developments and caught up with INTC and gained some market share.

Considering the latest developments, INTC's extremely poor performance and problematic processors in the last 2 generations will be a lifeline for AMD, which now has the market share advantage in the desktop CPU market. I think AMD's relative competitiveness, especially in desktop CPUs, improved with each generation.

I can see the financial reflection of this development in the distribution of revenue by segment. AMD's client segment revenue, which includes desktop, laptop, etc. CPUs, grew by 58.32% YoY. That's why I can see that the company's market share gain against INTC is also reflected positively in revenue.

Roadmap of Innovation

The reason I've been so pessimistic so far is because I've been putting market movements into words. AMD's technological progress has been lagging behind its competitors and pushed the company down by as much as 58% since mid-2024.

The question I want to answer here is this. If the company can develop much more advanced and better competitive technologies in the coming periods, then $96 will be a cheap price for AMD. That's why I want to examine the company's roadmap and expectations.

CES 2025: A Showcase of AI and Gaming Innovation

At CES 2025, we had the opportunity to see AMD's innovations in AI and mobile computing. AMD introduced its Ryzen AI Max Series processors. As the name suggests, the processor is focused on AI and includes an XDNA 2 neural processing unit (NPU) that provides 50+ tera operations per second (TOPS) specifically for AI workloads.

These processors aim to deliver near-desktop performance to premium laptops while also providing seamless multitasking for AI-powered applications like chatbots and image rendering. In addition to that, Ryzen AI 300 and 200 series have been announced as more affordable, mainstream processors.

While NVDA doesn't have much of a competitive edge in AI, AMD's Instinct MI300 series accelerators have gained some momentum in AI infrastructure, with partners like Oracle and Vultr adopting their MI300X GPUs for cloud services. There's a slight problem here. Wolfe Research estimates data center GPU sales in 2025 to be $7 billion, well below estimates. The MI325X, which launched in the months leading up to 2025, and the MI350, which will be released in mid-2025, aim to close this gap. While the MI350 promises a 35x improvement in AI inference performance over its predecessor, it's a mystery whether this gap will ever close.

Financials

First of all, I'd like to talk about the distribution of AMD's revenue by segment. I have already mentioned that there is a 58.32% growth YoY in the client segment which accounts for 30.20% of the revenue. In addition to that, there is a 69.11% increase YoY in the data center revenue, which accounts for 50.39% of the company's revenue.

But there are clear declines in gaming and embedded segment revenues. Even though it does not constitute a large portion of the revenue, there is a 58.85% decrease in the gaming segment, which constitutes 7.35% of the revenue. This is an indication that despite gaining good market share, AMD has not made significant progress in the low-budget graphics card market. Finally, there is a 12.68% decrease in the embedded segment.

With the company's entry into the AI ​​sector, data center revenues have also increased at the same rate. This increase was also reflected in quarterly revenues. AMD's revenue, which was stagnant and stable at a certain level since 2022, started to increase QoQ again as of 2024. In its latest press release company stated that they expect $7.1 billion in revenue for the next quarter (there is a negative and positive $300 million deviation margin). So there is an expectation of a 24.25% - 35.21% increase in revenue YoY.

This expectation also means a decline from quarter to quarter, but this is due to seasonality. AMD CEO Lisa Su expects the data center segment to decline in line with the enterprise average while the consumer and gaming segments are expected to decline due to seasonal factors. She also emphasized that the decline in the segment in particular will slow down and stabilize.

I would like to analyze in more detail what the CEO means by seasonal decline, based on the company's qualitative developments. The concept of the "corporate average" is often used in financial analysis to provide a benchmark against which individual business segments can be compared. It represents a typical or standard measure of performance for the entire company, in this case, since the company has an expected 7% sequential decline in overall revenue, the decline in this segment will be in line with this.

Q1 2025 Q4 2024 Q1 2024 Q4 2023 Q1 2023 Q4 2022
Data Center $3,590.00 $3,859.00 $2,337.00 $2,282.00 $1,295.00 $1,655.00
Client $2,029.34 $2,313.00 $1,368.00 $1,461.00 $739.00 $903.00
Gaming $464.48 $563.00 $922.00 $1,368.00 $1,757.00 $1,644.00
Embedded $885.38 $923.00 $846.00 $1,057.00 $1,562.00 $1,397.00

Outside of data centers, there is an average of 12.26% and 4.08% revenue decline between the last and first quarters, respectively, due to seasonality in the client and embedded segments. I expect a decline of this magnitude again. It has been stated that the decline will slow down in the gaming segment compared to the last decline of 32.60%, so I expect a decline between 20-15% (average 17.5%).

As a result, my personal revenue forecast for the next quarter is $6.96 billion. That's about $140 million below AMD's own revenue forecast of $7.1 billion, but it's in line with the company's $300 million margin of error. I think this revenue is pretty healthy for the company going forward.

Economic Profit

Economic profitability, unlike accounting profit, is a calculation method that measures whether the stock has yielded more than investors expected based on the risks associated with the investment.

Unfortunately, AMD did not pass my test in this regard. In order to buy a company with peace of mind, I want to make sure that its economic profitability is above 0%. The value being above 0% means that the company is not only growing, but also growing in a profitable way. However, AMD's economic profitability, although slow since mid-2023, is still at -9.32%. So this is not a very positive situation.

Forward P/E Ratio

Since 2017, the company's forward P/E ratio has generally been higher than NASDAQ 100's (NDX) forward P/E. However, this has changed especially after 2024, and AMD is priced lower than the NASDAQ in terms of forward P/E. Considering that the company's historical average is 34.6x forward P/E, the company is currently in one of its cheapest positions in history. Although economic profitability does not present a positive outlook, the forward P/E shows that the stock is quite cheap compared to its history and the index it is in.

Charting

First of all, I want to talk about the company's price performance in the last 10 years. AMD stock closed 7 of the last 10 years with profit and 3 years with a loss. Its average return was 74.13%. That's why I can say that the stock's price performance in the last 10 years is not bad.

I will use various indicators and price action methods for detailed analysis. First of all, stock is really in a critical area. This $88 level is the area where the stock has found the most volume of purchases since 2021. So it is quite logical to expect the stock to hold around this area.

When I look at the RSI, I see that it is in the 31.92 (oversold) area. Even though moving averages are broken, and the stock seems to have entered a bear market, it is quite possible for it to hold at this level and rise to $120 (200 Week WMA).

But that doesn't mean I expect the stock to re-enter the bull market and reach its previous high. The stock needs to break at least the 200-week WMA and the price action resistance level of $120 and close above it for the week before I can expect another bull run. Unless that happens, I will continue to hold the stock because it could continue to oscillate between $120-88.

Risks

The biggest risk for AMD, as for any tech stock right now, is the Trump Tariffs. As it stands, semiconductors received a limited exemption from the first wave of tariffs, but that exemption was narrowly defined and excluded many chip-related products. The tariff landscape is particularly worrisome for AMD because the company generates most of its revenue from international markets, which makes it very exposed to trade policies.

AMD's advanced chips are also manufactured by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC). The fact that Taiwan is facing a 32% tariff further increases this risk. President Trump has made it clear that chip-specific tariffs are "coming soon" which implies that the current semiconductor exemptions are temporary. So I think a company like AMD which is all about chips can negatively be impacted by these tariffs in the short to medium term at least.

In addition to that, investors have increasingly turned away from big technology stocks following the U.S. presidential election by turning to sectors that are believed to benefit more directly from the new administration's policies, such as banking and cryptocurrency, which have negative effects. This rotation has increased selling pressure on semiconductor stocks such as AMD.

There is uncertainty surrounding AMD's operations in China, not just in the U.S. As U.S.-China relations become increasingly tense, potential restrictions on AMD's ability to sell AI chips in the Chinese market continue to be a constant concern for investors.

Conclusion

AMD's late entry into the AI ​​sector has created a significant disadvantage, but the company continues to compete with NVDA without interruption. Although it made positive developments by taking the CPU market from Intel (INTC) to a great extent, it is still lagging behind in the technology sector in general.

I think the company is in a very good position in terms of hardware, but it has shortcomings in terms of software. It needs to take the AI ​​and Data Center issue seriously and create its own ecosystem as soon as possible. In addition to that, since NVDA has reduced its weight in the affordable graphics card market on the gaming side, AMD should evaluate this opportunity and pay attention to the software of its graphics cards to be more aggressive in the competition.

In this process, while the economic profitability analysis gives a negative result, forward P/E ratio and charting shows that the stock may be cheap historically and compared to the sector. That's why in this complex environment I prefer to stay on the "hold" side.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Fairly Valued, But Recession-Resilient
data.description:
At the beginning of January 2025, I wrote my last article about Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and in the article I rated the stock as a “Buy”. I argued that Johnson & Johnson’s stock could serve as a high-yield bond alternative and in the conclusion, I wrote:

In about two weeks, on January 22, 2025, the company will report fourth quarter results for fiscal 2024. Not only are analysts rather bullish right now with no “Sell” and “Strong Sell” ratings in the last 90 days and only one “Hold” rating, but we could argue that the downside is limited, and the stock price might not drop much lower than $135. On the other hand, we should also not expect high-growth rates or the stock price going to the moon. Johnson & Johnson could be a solid investment, paying a decent dividend yield and rather serve as a high-yield bond alternative.

The call was right as the stock increased about 20% in value in the following weeks. But similar to almost every other stock in the world, Johnson & Johnson declined in value in the last few days. Nevertheless, the stock still increased about 4% (and 5% when including dividends) while the S&P 500 declined 8.5% in the same timeframe.

Before Johnson & Johnson will report first quarter results for fiscal 2025 on Tuesday, April 15, 2025, let’s look at the company and stock once again. Especially considering how volatile the stock market was in the last few days and what implications tariffs and a potential recession might have, an update about Johnson & Johnson seems necessary.

Quarterly Results and Full-Year Results

Before we look at the potential future, let’s look back at the last results Johnson & Johnson reported on January 22, 2025, for the fourth quarter and full-year fiscal 2024. In the fourth quarter, sales as well as earnings per share did slightly beat estimates and when looking at the results year-over-year, sales increased from $21,395 million in Q4 '23 to $22,520 million in Q4 '24 – resulting in 5.3% year-over-year growth. But while sales increased, earnings before provision for taxes on income declined by 19.5% year-over-year from $4,826 million in the same quarter last year to $3,887 million in this quarter. Earnings per diluted share declined from $1.70 in Q4 '23 to $1.41 in Q4 '24 – resulting in a 17.1% year-over-year decline.

While we see Johnson & Johnson struggling a little bit in the fourth quarter (especially with operating income and earnings per share declining), full-year results are better. Sales increased from $85,159 million in fiscal 2023 to $88,821 million in fiscal 2024 – resulting in 4.1% year-over-year growth. Earnings before provision for taxes on income increased 10.8% year-over-year from $15,062 million in fiscal 2023 to $16,687 million in fiscal 2024. And diluted earnings per share from continuing operations increased from $5.20 in fiscal 2023 to $5.79 in fiscal 2024 – resulting in 11.3% year-over-year growth.

Johnson & Johnson is reporting in two major segments – Innovative Medicine, which generated $56,964 million in revenue in fiscal 2024 (64% of total revenue) and MedTech, which generated $31,857 million in fiscal 2024 (36% of total revenue). And both segments reported growth rates in the mid-single digits in fiscal 2024. When looking at the sub-segments, especially Oncology and Cardiovascular are worth mentioning and reporting high-growth rates.

Oncology generated $20,781 million in revenue in fiscal 2024 and the sub-segment grew revenue 17.7% year-over-year. Cardiovascular generated $7,707 million in fiscal 2024 and the sub-segment grew its revenue 21.4% year-over-year.

Growth

Johnson & Johnson is certainly not a high-growth business, and investors should not expect high-growth rates from Johnson & Johnson. And the problem becomes obvious when looking at the different segments. While Johnson & Johnson is growing at a very high pace in its two sub-segments oncology and cardiovascular, but since other sub-segments are not growing, the top line isn’t increasingly rapidly.

And management is also optimistic for fiscal 2025, but adjusted operational sales will grow only between 2.0% and 3.0%. Operational sales are expected to be in a range of $90.9 billion and $91.7 billion. Adjusted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $10.50 to $10.70, resulting in 5.2% to 7.2% year-over-year growth.

For the first quarter of fiscal 2025, analysts are also not expecting Johnson & Johnson to grow at a high pace. Revenue is expected to be $21.59 billion, which would result in about 1% top-line growth compared to the same quarter last year. Earnings per share (on a GAAP basis) are expected to be $2.27 and, compared to $1.34 in the same quarter last year, it would actually result in a high-growth rate. But in my opinion, we should rather look at the adjusted earnings per share of $2.29 in the same quarter last year and in this case, the bottom-line would slightly decline.

Analysts have similar expectations for the years to come and assume earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 4.1% between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2033. And I don’t think we have much reason to be more optimistic. I already mentioned above that Johnson & Johnson can grow in the double-digits in some parts of its business – for example oncology and cardiovascular – but due to the large and very diversified business it is not enough when one or two sub-segments are growing with a high pace. It is also not enough for a business generating $90 billion in revenue to introduce two or three new blockbuster drugs. And Johnson & Johnson certainly has a promising pipeline.

Aside from growing by improving the top line, Johnson & Johnson – like every other business – can also grow by lowering expenses and improving efficiency. But I don’t think we should expect margin improvement to contribute in a significant way to bottom-line growth. I think it is possible for slightly higher margins to contribute about 1% growth annually, but we should not be too optimistic. Johnson & Johnson was trading for stable margins in the last few decades (which is actually a good sign for stability).

Additionally, Johnson & Johnson can use share buybacks to grow the bottom line and, in the past, the company has used share buybacks – but it was never a big part of its capital allocation strategy. During the last 20 years, the number of shares outstanding was reduced with a CAGR of 1.14%, and we can assume that share buybacks will contribute about 1% to bottom-line growth.

Overall, it is difficult to imagine that Johnson & Johnson will grow at a higher rate than in the low-to-mid single digits. In the last ten years, Johnson & Johnson grew its revenue with a CAGR of 1.80%. Operating income, however, increased only with a CAGR of 0.47% and earnings per share grew only 0.16% annually in the last ten years.

Recession

Looking at the growth rates and upside potential is certainly important, but we should also not ignore the flip side – the downside risk a business is facing. And not only due to the tariffs announced in the last few days we should always consider what impact a potential recession might have on a business.

When looking at the stock price performance of Johnson & Johnson in the last few decades, we see several drawdowns, but the steepest decline was in the 1970s when the stock fell 52.5% from its previous all-time high. All following corrections and bear markets took down Johnson & Johnson only about 40% or less and compared to many other stocks this is rather moderate.

Especially when looking at the S&P 500 in comparison, we see Johnson & Johnson performing much better in most cases. And performing better can have many different meanings: Usually it means that an investment in a certain stock is leading to a higher return and Johnson & Johnson slightly outperformed the S&P 500 over the last 30 years when including dividends.

But right now, the decisive question is not whether a stock can outperform the index during the bull run, the question right now is how we can limit the downside risk during bear markets and a potential recession. And Johnson & Johnson as a healthcare business might be able to perform well again during a downturn.

When looking at revenue as well as earnings per share in the last decades, we can’t claim that Johnson & Johnson was not affected at all by a recession, but it is performing solid. When excluding the years 2020 and the following, revenue hardly declined and earnings per share declined steeply on several occasions – but in almost no case we can make a connection between the declining earnings per share and a recession.

The argument for healthcare and pharmaceutical companies is very simple: The products Johnson & Johnson is selling are not only essential, but in some cases even essential for survival. Of course, surgeries can sometimes be postponed or in some cases it is possible to switch to cheaper alternatives for some pharmaceuticals. Nevertheless, a major part of Johnson & Johnson’s revenue can be seen as almost guaranteed.

I think we will see the fiscal guidance of countless companies being revised and analysts will also lower targets. In the case of Johnson & Johnson, we can be optimistic that the company will be able to meet its fiscal 2025 guidance.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

An important step in every analysis is to determine an intrinsic value for a stock to reach a decision if a stock is over- or undervalued. And for a first quick overview, I usually look at the price-free-cash-flow ratio and the price-earnings ratio. In the case of Johnson & Johnson, both valuation multiples indicate that Johnson & Johnson is not really expensive, but it is also not cheap or a bargain.

At the time of writing, Johnson & Johnson is trading for 25.6 times earnings, which seems rather expensive for a business growing only in the low-to-mid single digits, and it is also above the 5-year average of 23.10. When looking at the P/FCF ratio, Johnson & Johnson is trading for a valuation multiple of 19.95, which seems a little more acceptable and is also below the 5-year average of 22.63.

Especially with the recent crash, we can find several other stocks trading for similar valuation multiples – but these stocks are often growing at a much higher pace.

As always, we are not only looking at these simple valuation multiples but also use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock. Once again, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate as this is the minimum annual return we like to achieve. Additionally, we calculate with 2,429 million diluted shares outstanding and the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $18.06 billion.

Calculating with these assumptions, Johnson & Johnson has to grow its free cash flow slightly below 5% annually from now till perpetuity to be fairly valued. And considering the growth assumptions we made above, we can argue that these growth rates are reasonable, but I would also not assume that Johnson & Johnson is able to grow at a much higher pace.

However, I would argue that the free cash flow of the last four quarters might be a little bit too pessimistic as a basis. In the last four quarters, JNJ reported a free cash flow margin of 20.3% (meaning 20.3% of revenue ended up as free cash flow). The average of the last ten years, however, was 22.4%, and we can assume some margin improvement here. When assuming that 22.4% of fiscal 2025 revenue (according to guidance) will end up as free cash flow and assuming 5% growth from now till perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of $168.39, and we can see Johnson & Johnson as slightly undervalued at this point. However, it is also possible that 5% annual growth till perpetuity is a little too optimistic for Johnson & Johnson over the long run.

Conclusion

I would argue that Johnson & Johnson can be seen as fairly valued, and it is certainly not the perfect investment. But in a time of very high levels of uncertainty, limited stability and high volatility on the stock market, Johnson & Johnson might be a business worth considering.

I still am not expecting high-growth rates from Johnson & Johnson in the years to come, and the stock is certainly not a stock likely to double or triple in a few years. But I consider the stock as an investment that will provide some level of stability in volatile times and an investment with limited downside risk. Johnson & Johnson, as one of the major healthcare and pharmaceutical businesses might be more recession-resilient than many other businesses and over the next few years, this might actually be one of the most important criteria for any investment.

data.title: IGD: Capital Erosion And Shrinking Dividend Payouts
data.description:
Overview

Voya Global Equity Dividend and Premium Opportunity Fund (NYSE:IGD) operates as a diversified option ETF that aims to provide attractive total returns and provide shareholders with a high level of income. I've grown to enjoy the stability and income that option ETFs can provide. However, I've realized that not all option ETFs are constructed the same. IGD has an inception dating back to 2005, which means we have over two decades worth of performance data to reference. Additionally, the ETF has a reasonable expense ratio of 0.97%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that IGD's price has fallen by about 0.9%, with most of the decline happening over the last few weeks. When including all the distributions that have been paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 10.4% over the same period. One of the main appeals of a fund like IGD is the generous starting dividend yield of 11.7%. Distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, which can make it an attractive option for retired investors seeking a supplemental form of dividend income. However, the high income comes with inherent flaws that make me cautious about the long-term performance of the fund.

Although the income from IGD may be attractive, the fund has a poor history of consistently eroding investor capital. Additionally, the most recent reporting documents indicate that the underlying earnings aren't enough to actually support the distributions. Most of the distributions come from return of capital, which can expedite NAV decay when markets are declining. So let's start by taking a look at IGD's underlying fund strategy that it implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's overview, IGD has net assets of $488M that are spread across 237 different holdings. The fund takes a global approach and focuses on companies that have a history of attractive dividend yields. The global exposure can be a great way to slowly diversify your portfolio away from the US indexes. 69% of the fund's assets are still US-based, but there are varying amounts of capital invested across Asia and Europe.

We can see that investments in Financials comprise the largest portion of the fund at 25.77%. This is followed by investments in Industrials and Health Care companies, accounting for 15.06% and 13.71% of assets respectively. However, the combination of these three sectors comprise over 54% of total assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on IGD's daily price changes and total return potential.

The fund doesn't explicitly state that it puts a focus on dividend growth, but some underlying holdings indicate that it's an important part of the strategy. For instance, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) is the largest position within IGD, with a weight of 1.66% of assets. For reference, JNJ has been able to raise its dividend for 62 consecutive years without disruption. This is followed by positions in The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) and AbbVie Inc. (ABBV), accounting for 1.65% and 1.60% of assets respectively. PG is another dividend king who has raised dividends for 68 consecutive years without disruption. Just for reference, here are some notable dividend traits of the top holdings:

  • RTX Corporation (RTX): 31 consecutive years of dividend raises with a payout ratio of only 43%.
  • PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP): 52 consecutive years of dividend raises with a 5-year dividend CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 7.25%.

However, I can't help but think that the focus on dividend growth is a bit obsolete here since IGD chooses to implement an option overlay strategy. This means that management writes options against portions of their underlying holdings in an effort to generate additional income for shareholders. These options produce premiums, which are used to fund the distributions. The fund reporting seems to indicate that IGD deploys an OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategy, which allows for a greater amount of upside potential. However, the growth is ultimately capped to the selected strike price of the option. Therefore, I expect IGD to underperform traditional ETFs that track indices like the S&P 500 (SPX).

Valuation & Risk Profile

The inclusion of an option strategy is one of the contributing factors to IGD's price decline. Looking at the performance of IGD since inception, the share price has declined by over 74.4%. The fund has decimated investor's capital and is a clear indication that the underlying strategy may be ineffective. Even though the total return when including distributions is now above 122%, I do not believe this is worth the risk of seeing your invested capital erode over time. The history of eroding capital is what ultimately stops me from suggesting this fund over a long time horizon.

In a hypothetical scenario, if an investor committed $10,000 into IGD, their position would now be valued at approximately $2,500. However, the investor would have received a little over $12,000 back in distributions over the two decades. Two decades is a long time to wait to get your initial capital back.

IGD operates as a closed-end fund so that prices can trade at a difference from the actual value of underlying assets. As a result, the price currently trades at a discount to NAV of 8.6%. Looking back over the last decade below, IGD has exclusively traded in the discount territory and has never crossed into the premium to NAV territory, even through bull markets. This could be an indication that the market doesn't deem IGD as a valuable fund. In an effort to determine whether or not this discount to NAV pricing is an opportunity, I took a look at different reporting documents.

The most up-to-date report is the annual report dated from August 2024. The fund generates its earnings through a combination of net investment income and net realized gains from its underlying holdings. The report indicates that the biggest risk would be that IGD has failed to generate a sufficient amount of earnings to fully support distributions. This has ultimately led to the fund's NAV decreasing over the last decade. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2015: $9.31
  • 2016: $7.52
  • 2017: $8.01
  • 2018: $8.03
  • 2019: $7.02
  • 2020: $6.26
  • 2021: $6.01
  • 2022: $6.36
  • 2023: $5.97
  • 2024: $6.12

The declining NAV is the clearest indication that the strategy is ultimately ineffective over a long stretch of time. While there were some slight upticks on the NAV for some years, they weren't enough to ultimately change the momentum to a positive sentiment.

However, I will give IGD credit where it's due. The fund has proven to be more resilient during downturns because of the option strategy. For instance, markets declined throughout 2022 because of a Fed rate shock. The Fed aggressively increased interest rates to combat record-high inflationary data. Markets declined and traditional index-tracking ETFs such as SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ). For instance, SPY saw a total loss of 18.17% and QQQ experienced a loss of 32.58%. In comparison, IGD was a lot more resilient and had a total loss of only 5%.

Dividend Decreases

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.05 per share, the current dividend yield sits at around 11.7%. If we multiply the current monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.60 per share. However, the dividend history over the last decade clearly indicates that payouts have continued to decrease over time. This leads me to believe that the payouts are ultimately too generous, and the fund could benefit from a reduction.

While management has reduced payouts over time, I still don't think the reductions have been strong enough. The fund is in a bit of a difficult spot because if they severely reduce distributions, it sort of defeats the purpose of the fund. If they don't reduce the payouts, the NAV may continue to deteriorate over the next decade if changes aren't made.

This is reinforced by the latest section 19(a) notice that indicates 90% of the distributions have been funded by return of capital, while only 10% of the distributions were actually funded by net investment income. Dependence on return of capital can indicate that IGD hasn't actually been able to generate earnings that support the distribution. The only benefit to return of capital is that the distributions aren't actually taxed. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced and taxes are deferred until the time of sale.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate IGD as a sell because of the continued capital erosion, NAV declines, and shrinking dividend payouts. While the fund does have exposure to high-quality companies, the distribution policy appears to be too generous. The large distributions have caused the fund's NAV to decay over time. While management continues to decrease payouts, it hasn't really helped the fund regain some positive momentum in its NAV. I do not believe the high dividend yield is worth the risks here.
