data.title: CoreWeave: One Round Before It's Game Over (Upgrading To Buy)
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:CRWV) stock chart resembles a kindergarten-written "M," shown in the following chart of the stock since going public in late March against the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nebius Group (NBIS). The stock shot down on the day of its IPO, Friday, by around 7%, then surged around 65% between Monday and Wednesday from $37.80 to $61.36 per share. Since its 52-week-high of $64.62 per share, the stock has crashed over 31%, specifically getting dragged down on Thursday, with the stock closing over 13% lower for the day.

We're upgrading CoreWeave to a buy for the short term; we called sell on the stock after its IPO, and our bearish call played out pretty well, as shown below, with the stock down 12% versus the S&P 500 up roughly 4%.

Our bearish thesis of renting GPUs as a service business model remains valid for the mid-to-long run, but we think the stock could outperform in the near-term because of the logic of IPOs.

CoreWeave management, while yes, finance guys rather than tech guys, wouldn't take the company public if they don't have the pipeline to support growth. So, our thesis is about a structural flaw for this neo-cloud peer group in the longer term is still in play. However, CoreWeave, in particular, should be okay for the near term, considering this current continued optimism and the fact that the current negatives have been recognized by the market. For now, where AI is concerned, all the tier 1 guys are holding on to their 2025 cloud capex. The structural problem with CoreWeave and its peers, i.e., Nebius Group, Crusoe, Lambda Labs, or Together AI, is that these companies have to keep spending, or more formally, they need to have upfront capex, or they are dead. If they don't spend overtime, revenue will drop. We think that, in the short term, the pipeline to support growth is there, and the stock should trade higher.

The reason we specify consistent spend to see revenue growth is that when it comes to renting out GPUs, they can only slap a higher average selling price or ASP when the GPU is fresh, i.e., they quickly lose ASP power over time on older tech. The only players that can afford to keep up that higher spend are the tier 1 guys, so while Microsoft is now a top customer, it will end up being CoreWeave's competitor and Amazon's (AMZN) AWS is already a competitor listed on SemiAnalysis' chart. Now, tariffs make things worse because tariffs going up means expenses will go up, and spending is already a point of concern for CoreWeave, with a debt burden of $8B. But, this also shouldn't weigh on the coming quarter, so we think for now this is an attractive swing trade.

It's all about the near-term pipeline

Now, our buy call is for a swing trade, not a long-term position. The selling point, or more accurately, buying point, for CoreWeave now is the idea that the company has a strong pipeline for the near-term. The rumors about Microsoft (MSFT) cutting or backing off of deals have yet to be confirmed, so they signal future troubles rather than near-term ones. And, anyway, the market has priced them in. The news about Google (GOOGL)(GOOG) being close to agreeing with CoreWeave on a deal, reported by The Information, could also be a booster if confirmed on this quarter's earnings call.

The company also announced a $4.5B deal and 15-year lease agreement with Galaxy Digital "to host its infrastructure at the latter's Helios campus in West Texas." Galaxy Digital has basically changed itself from a crypto mine data center to host CoreWeave as an AI provider. The deal basically means that Galaxy will give 133MW of critical IT Load to host AI and HPC infrastructure for Corewweave, and all capacity should be set up by the end of 1H26. There is also the deal closed with OpenAI in March on the table, which further highlights that this is the go-to guy with the neoclouds. So, we think there is trouble for the neocloud in the midterm, but we think CoreWeave is in a well trafficked area for the short term, so it should be okay.

Our thesis on Tech Contrarians remains to see a pullback from the tier 1 capex spend for the year, and that includes CoreWeave's largest customer, Microsoft, but the fact remains that Microsoft is holding on to that $80B figure for the year. Google just yesterday reiterated its $75B despite panic on AI demand compared to spend. On its S-1, the company reported remaining performance obligations or RPOs of $15.1B. CoreWeave also got higher visibility than its competition when it comes to clients, and that should show up in the coming earnings report.

Our advice is to buy the stock below $42 levels, which we think we'll see happen, and step out once the stock surges on its near-term pipeline, considering market optimism on the stock is waning, so a boost in confidence shouldn't be impossible for the current quarter's report. The insider trading activity seems to be doing a similar swing trade strategy, although, on a much shorter time span, the following outlines the trading activity news headlines on April 5th.

What we emphasize is that this is a name that is likely to see higher losses in the mid to long run due to its need and lack of upfront capex, high debt, and high dependence on older generation Nvidia (NVDA) AI GPUs. Once that near-term boost happens, that's when this turns into an attractive short opportunity. We'll downgrade CRWV stock once we see that pop because we think they'll win one round before it's game over.

data.title: Ethereum Needs A Defining Catalyst (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
So far this year, Ethereum (ETH-USD) has erased the gains it made in 2024 and has been on the hardest receiving end (among major altcoins) of the crypto sell-off we've witnessed in the past month. For comparison, on a YTD basis, ETH has lost ~55%, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has lost ~16%, Solana (SOL-USD) is down by ~42%, while Ripple (XRP-USD) lost ~15%.

The spot Ethereum ETFs approved in May last year were a significant milestone for ETH; however, they've not had nearly as much demand as the spot Bitcoin ETFs. On the surface, this isn't surprising because Bitcoin has the strongest community and track record among crypto assets. When investors hold Bitcoin, they feel they own an asset that is widely regarded as a solid store of value. At least the U.S. government has said they won't be selling any more Bitcoin and plan to buy Bitcoin in the future. This cements investors’ confidence, at least for now, and might be a good incentive to hold BTC, especially in the current market turmoil.

Turning to the second most valued crypto, Ethereum, some key questions to revisit include: why should investors hold ETH or its spot ETFs? What incentives do investors have? With the market retrace we've seen so far this year, are there any catalysts left for Ethereum or developments in the pipeline that would incentivize investors to purchase and hold ETH or its ETPs? Let's get into these analyses in this article.

Staking Integration Into Spot Ethereum ETF Is A Potential Catalyst

At this point, ETH needs a strong catalyst to move the needle. The market generally lacks momentum right now, and I'm not overweighting ETH at this time, and think investors should monitor ETH sentiment closely. The YTD ETH-BTC chart doesn't look good. It is down ~47%

Amid this downturn, what could potentially move the needle for ETH by creating an incentive to buy and hold the asset and in turn create a lasting trend reversal not just dead cat bounces? I think staking integration into the spot Ethereum ETFs is one potential catalyst to watch out for.

The current SEC leadership has taken a more lenient approach toward crypto regulation, and I’d say it’s working to ease the bad blood between blockchain firms and regulators. There have been some surprising moves by the government and regulators lately, like the release of Ross Ulbricht, and the SEC dropping its investigations into certain crypto firms. The appeal to the XRP case was dropped by the SEC. This is just one instance of closed litigation. There have also been dismissals of some long-standing enforcement actions, like the enforcement action against Kraken and Consensys were dropped weeks ago. These concessions are great for the industry and send the signal that clarity is likely coming for every aspect of crypto that has had regulatory ambiguity.

The latest SEC statement that directly influences the industry as a whole and shows how crypto regulation is shaping up, is the declaration that certain Proof of Work (“PoW”) activities and cryptos earned from proof of work mechanisms or participation in mining pools do not constitute securities offerings under the federal securities laws. This clarity is a big relief for PoW cryptocurrencies and miners.

Now turning to the PoW cousin which is the Proof of Stake (“PoS”), which powers Ethereum and other major blockchains like Solana, there is need for a similar clarity surrounding PoS chains, and staking in particular. Such clarity will be one of the catalysts for a potential ETH rebound when market sentiment becomes much better and the downturn caused by the tariffs war blows over.

The topic of PoS staking triggering security laws has become an even hotter debate since the spot Ethereum ETF fund managers began filing amendments for staking approval in the ETFs. There have been several filings by the fund managers, pushing for staking approval. NYSE filed the latest amendment the previous week on behalf of Bitwise asking the SEC to allow staking in the Bitwise spot Ethereum ETF (ETHW). Just a slight digression here, I recently wrote about how Bitwise is in a better position to capture ETH ETF share if staking gets approved, because of their existing robust ETH staking infrastructure and past expertise in running an ETH staking ETP. You can read the ETHW article here.

The big question remains, will a similar clarity that has come for PoW chains likely come for PoS chains in the near future? Are there any inherent constraints to PoS networks that make such approval NOT remotely possible?

I’ll dive into some points in this piece, based on my analysis and perspective, and why I think there could be reconsiderations from regulators regarding PoS chains and PoS activities, and some arguments I think the ETH ETF fund managers will lean into, which will maybe lead to a potential staking approval in the ETFs, not also forgetting some valid counterpoints that would strengthen regulators' argument and stance. This article is meant to be an open conversation. Other supporting or opposing views are more than welcome; let’s make it a real discussion in the comments. The topic of ETF staking approval is still a bit of a gray area, even the current SEC leadership hasn’t given any clear signal yet, but that’s exactly what makes it worth digging into and what currently piques the curiosity of crypto investors.

Putting Ethereum Proof-of-Stake Through the Howey Test

There will probably be plenty of angles for ETH ETF filers to argue that staking shouldn’t trigger securities laws. The same goes for the SEC, which has precedent to argue that the pooling of assets and expectation of yield (staking rewards in this case), are enough to deny staking in the spot ETFs.

The Howey Test centers on four criteria - 1. an investment of money, 2. in a common enterprise, 3. with an expectation of profit, 4. primarily from the efforts of others. How will the fund managers circumvent the notion of expectation of profit, reliance on a common enterprise, and the centralization of validator operations (efforts of others)?

To get staking approved, I believe some staking structures will be off the table from the get-go. For example, Ethereum’s liquid staking, which has gained popularity since introduced after the Merge and has seen TVL grow strongly because of the advantages of liquidity and DeFi composability, will likely not be viable for consideration at all. The reason being liquid staking tokens represent a rebaseable or yield-bearing claim on staked ETH that is pooled and managed by third parties, which fits the SEC’s definition of an investment contract under Howey’s criteria of “reliance on a common enterprise.”

The fund managers would likely strategize to go all decentralized in their staking structure. This will greatly reduce counterparty risks, and such risk would be one of the core arguments for the SEC to deny approval of the staking. Bitwise is worth a second mention here, being the only issuer so far with an in-house staking structure that could probably be integrated seamlessly into the spot ETF structure. Fidelity (FETH) also self-custodies its ETH holdings for the spot Ethereum ETF, but Fidelity hasn't announced any in-house staking infrastructure yet.

To evade triggering the Howey criteria, the issuers might argue that investors are only putting money into ETH itself via the spot ETFs, and not directly into staking. The staking process is secondary to the investor’s core objective, which is exposure to ETH’s spot price. The fact that staking rewards are tacked on is more like a side benefit, just like dividends on stocks. The real asset the investor is backing is the underlying commodity, ETH, and staking isn’t their primary investment strategy. The rewards from staking would simply be passed through to investors in proportion to their shareholding. There is really no common enterprise beyond the primary function of managing the spot ETF. The funds aren’t running a business that actively profits from the staking pool, they just hold ETH and secure the Ethereum network technologically.

Staking is a technological necessity for the Ethereum network. And staking rewards aren't exactly speculative profit - it’s a reward for “work done,” which is to secure Ethereum’s network. Staking isn’t primarily about “making money” but about preserving the value of ETH by sustaining the network.

Risks and Conclusion

The preceding arguments are based on my interpretation and analysis of ETH within the Howey framework. While they highlight some passable arguments the issuers could present to secure approval of staking in the ETFs, the SEC also has precedent on its side and has long argued that staking yields from centralized operations trigger securities laws. However, with the current U.S. administration’s stance towards crypto and their seriousness about removing ambiguity surrounding crypto assets and their regulatory frameworks (at least from recent press releases, this has been their stance), I think an early path to getting such approval isn't totally out of reach.

On the flip side, even a staking integration in ETH spot ETFs might turn out not to be the needle-moving catalyst we highly anticipate. ETH staking rewards are currently ~3.4% APY, compared to a PoS blockchain like Solana with ~8.17% APY. Issuers have begun filing for spot Solana ETFs too. And if Solana sees an early spot ETF approval (with the current crypto-friendly SEC leadership, I'm not taking anything off the table), it means there will be intense competition among these major altcoins for spot ETF dominance. If by some chance staking gets approved for both Ethereum and Solana spot ETFs, Solana’s higher APY will likely mean diminishing appeal for Ethereum.

I feel Ethereum lacks a fresh, defining catalyst at the moment - the type we saw during the Merge and introduction of liquid staking in late 2022, which saw ETH rise from around $1,500 at the time of the Merge to over $4,000 in 2024. One of the main catalysts we look forward to at the moment - staking integration into the spot ETFs - isn't a sure thing yet. And even if it got greenlit, a ~3% APY might not be compelling enough for some investors, considering the volatility risk inherent in crypto. Investors might feel they are better off with REIT-focused ETFs that offer even higher yields.

Ethereum’s Pectra upgrade is forthcoming (in early May) and would potentially bring lower fees and faster transaction confirmation time to the Ethereum network - a good one for ETH. However, EIP 7251, a core part of the Pectra upgrade, which will increase the validators staking limits from 32 ETH to 2048 ETH, raises some centralization concerns, because we could witness consolidation of the smaller validators with 32 ETH staked, into fewer validators with 2048 staked ETH. Centralization concern is a big deal because it influences governance decisions on the network. And based on this, Ethereum may just be on its way out of being the trustless, decentralized network that offered the foundation for DeFi and decentralized apps, which has been the network's main selling points over the years.

data.title: SmartRent: Further Drop In Price Makes Us Load Up The Truck
data.description:
SmartRent (NYSE:SMRT) is a provider of smart home and smart property solutions for the multifamily industry. Since its IPO via SPAC in 2021, the company has had a challenging time with unclarity of its vision mounting in the CEO stepping down in mid-2024. After just 2 months with the new CEO Shane Paladin at the helm, the company just announced the resignation of this new CEO as well. Since our last article, the stock price massively dropped over 50%, putting the company deep into penny stock territory, primarily as a reaction to the CEO stepping down unexpectedly.

Since Our Last Article

Since our last article just two months ago, a lot has happened. SmartRent reported their Q4 2024 alongside their full year 2024 results.

While Hardware revenue sharply declined, the company's most important revenue stream, their SaaS revenue, continues to show strong growth at 26% y/y for the full year 2024. The growth in SaaS stems from a combination of rising SaaS ARPU (up 4% y/y) and rising number of units deployed (up 12% y/y).

For the full year 2024, the company has used around $33m cash in operating activities. While this cash burn is substantial, their revenue mix was much more healthy in 2024, with almost 42% of the company's revenue coming from Hosted Services in full year 2024.

This shift in revenue mix rhymes well with the company's renewed focus on SaaS revenue, with the incoming CEO Shane Paladin mentioning in the Q4 2024 call, there is a:

...significant opportunity to expand [recurring SaaS revenue]

Also, CFO Daryl Stemm said:

[SmartRent continues its] strategic transformation toward a SaaS focused business model.

Taking a look at their balance sheet, the company still has an impressive and healthy balance sheet, with over $142m in cash and a total of over $257m in current assets, compared to just $130m in total liabilities.

On 31st March, the company also published a press release announcing some AI-powered features in their smart operations system. In the announcement, SmartRent talks about three new features, and this further showcases their push in their SaaS offering.

The most impactful announcement, however, was the resignation of the CEO less than two months after onboarding the company. While the press release does not specify the reasons of the separation, it does say that the resignation happened "at the request of the board". Following this announcement, the share dropped almost 30% in a day to just 80 cents per share.

What To Look Out For

Given their elevated cash burn in 2024 due to costs in connection to a one-off legal matter and separation costs from the prior CEO, we assume that given their strong cash balance, SmartRent will have around 5 years to execute on their turnaround, providing sufficient runway and strong downside protection for investors.

Again, the company is looking for a CEO to steer its turnaround. We are looking out for any potential news as to the reason for the resignation of Paladin, and also for news surrounding a new CEO. Since the company managed to continue to grow their SaaS business even without a CEO at the helm before, we believe that the company can still execute the turnaround with interim-CEO John Dorman at the helm for now.

For the coming quarters, it is especially important to see if SmartRent can be successful in its quest to increase recurring SaaS revenue and improve efficiency. Hardware Revenue should increasingly be seen as a side-business for SmartRent to sell more SaaS contracts. While Hardware revenue still makes up the strongest revenue stream, its Gross Profit margin sat at under 30% in full-year 2024, compared to a SaaS profit margin of over 74%.

If SmartRent is able to significantly reignite SaaS growth with their latest efforts, we see a path to cash flow break-even by 2026.

Risks

The main risk, of course, is the potential inability of the company to continue growing their SaaS revenue or running out of cash before the break-even point. Aside from what, we believe, are obvious leadership issues, these risks could put the company out of business.

Also, tariffs on China could increase prices for smart home devices. We were unable to find any information on where a lot of the hardware SmartRent sells is produced, but we assume there could be some exposure to China. A stark increase in hardware prices could limit SmartRent's ability to grow SaaS revenue beyond already deployed units.

Valuation

Amid greater market uncertainty and a steep decline in share price, we continue to stick to our original valuation model. Citing a median ARR of 7.0x, ignoring SmartRent's hardware and professional services income stream, it would put SmartRent at a $420m valuation at a $60m ARR. This translates to a SMRT share price of just above $2.1, or an upside of around 200% to current prices.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
